skill.name: cellxgene-census
skill.description: Programmatically query the CZ CELLxGENE Census (61M+ cells) when you need cross-tissue, disease, or cell-type expression data for population-scale queries and reference atlas comparisons.
skill.summary: Query 61M+ single-cell records from the CZ CELLxGENE Census for cross-tissue and cross-disease expression analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 166 static analysis findings are false positives. This is a documentation skill containing markdown files with Python code examples for the cellxgene-census library. The HIGH findings for 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' refer to plain text mentions of 'raw count data' in bioinformatics documentation. The MEDIUM 'shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code fence markers and inline code formatting, not executable shell commands. The LOW 'system reconnaissance' findings flag biological data query functions. The LOW 'hardcoded URL' is a source repository citation. No malicious intent or executable threats detected.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Terms in Bioinformatic Documentation
low_findings.description: False positive. The term 'raw' in phrases like 'raw count data' and 'raw expression' is a standard bioinformatics term for unprocessed sequencing data. No cryptographic algorithms are present.

low_findings.title: Shell Backtick Confused with Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: False positive. Backticks in these markdown documentation files are code fence markers (```python, ```bash) and inline code formatting (`cellxgene_census`, `get_anndata()`). No Ruby shell execution is present.

low_findings.title: Biological Data Query Functions Flagged as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: False positive. Functions like get_obs(), get_anndata(), and axis_query() are cellxgene-census library functions for querying single-cell RNA sequencing data from a public biological database.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source Repository URL
low_findings.description: False positive. The URL at SKILL.md line 7 is a citation link to the skill source repository on GitHub. It is a legitimate attribution and not a malicious network endpoint.

content.user_title: Query single-cell gene expression from the CELLxGENE Census
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to access and query population-scale single-cell data across tissues, diseases, and cell types. This skill provides the patterns and reference documentation to programmatically query the CZ CELLxGENE Census with 61 million cells for cross-tissue comparisons and reference atlas lookups.
actual_capabilities: Query cell metadata from the CELLxGENE Census using expressive filter syntax for tissue, disease, and cell type.
Retrieve gene expression data as AnnData objects for small to medium sized queries.
Perform out-of-core expression analysis using SOMA axis_query for large datasets that exceed RAM.
Explore population-scale metadata summaries including cell counts by tissue, disease, and dataset.
Access versioned Census data for reproducible research with pinned release dates.
limitations: Requires internet access to query the remote CELLxGENE Census database.
Does not run analysis code directly; provides query patterns and reference for manual execution.
Cannot modify or write data back to the Census; only read operations are supported.
Large queries may require significant memory and out-of-core iteration strategies.

use_cases.title: Cross-tissue gene expression comparison
use_cases.description: A researcher compares macrophage expression patterns across lung, liver, and brain tissue to understand tissue-specific immune responses.

use_cases.title: Disease-specific cell type analysis
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician queries T cell expression data from COVID-19 patients to identify disease-associated gene signatures across multiple studies.

use_cases.title: Single-cell reference atlas validation
use_cases.description: A data scientist validates cell type markers from their own experiment against the Census reference to confirm expected expression patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Simple cell metadata query
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to find the number of brain cells available in the CELLxGENE Census for humans. Show me the Python code to open the Census, filter for brain tissue, and get the cell count.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner exploring what data is available in the Census.

prompt_templates.title: Gene expression retrieval for specific cell types
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to retrieve expression data for CD4 and CD8A genes in T cells from COVID-19 patient samples. Use get_anndata() with the proper obs_value_filter and var_value_filter.
prompt_templates.scenario: A researcher querying expression data for a targeted gene panel.

prompt_templates.title: Out-of-core analysis for large queries
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to compute the mean expression of FOXP2, TBR1, and SATB2 across all brain cells in the Census. The result will be too large for memory. Show me how to use axis_query() with chunked table iteration.
prompt_templates.scenario: A bioinformatician working with population-scale data exceeding available RAM.

prompt_templates.title: Reproducible cross-disease comparison workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a complete workflow to compare monocyte expression between COVID-19 and healthy control samples from the CELLxGENE Census. Pin a specific Census version for reproducibility. First explore available metadata, then retrieve expression data for key marker genes.
prompt_templates.scenario: A data scientist building a reproducible analysis pipeline for publication.

output_examples.input: What cell types are available in lung tissue from the CELLxGENE Census?
output_examples.output: The skill guides you through querying cell metadata with a filter for lung tissue. It returns a count of unique cell types such as alveolar epithelial cells, macrophages, fibroblasts, and endothelial cells, along with the number of cells per type.

output_examples.input: Get CD4 and CD8A expression in T cells from COVID-19 samples.
output_examples.output: The skill provides the code to retrieve an AnnData object containing 4,200 T cells from COVID-19 samples with expression values for CD4 and CD8A. The result includes metadata columns for cell type, tissue, disease, donor, and sex for downstream analysis in scanpy.

output_examples.input: Compare FOXP2 expression across brain regions in the Census.
output_examples.output: The skill demonstrates an out-of-core query using axis_query to iterate over brain cell data in chunks. It computes the mean expression of FOXP2 across returned entries without loading the full dataset into memory. Results show expression values per chunk with cell and gene coordinate mappings.

best_practices: Always include is_primary_data == True in filters to avoid double-counting cells that appear in multiple source datasets.
Use pinned Census versions (census_version="YYYY-MM-DD") to ensure reproducibility of analysis results.
Explore metadata with get_obs() before making expensive expression queries to confirm the data you need exists.
anti_patterns: Do not query expression data without first checking available cell counts by tissue and disease to avoid unexpectedly large results.
Do not use get_anndata() for queries expected to exceed 100,000 cells; use axis_query() with chunked iteration instead.
Do not omit is_primary_data filtering unless duplicates are explicitly needed for your analysis.

faq.question: What is the CELLxGENE Census?
faq.answer: It is a versioned collection of over 61 million single-cell RNA sequencing records from the CZ CELLxGENE Discover platform, built on the TileDB-SOMA framework.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need to install the cellxgene-census Python package using pip or uv. The skill provides the installation commands.

faq.question: Can I write data back to the Census?
faq.answer: No. The Census is a read-only public database. This skill only supports querying and retrieving existing data.

faq.question: How do I avoid duplicated cells in my results?
faq.answer: Add is_primary_data == True to your obs_value_filter. This ensures each cell appears only once even if it was contributed by multiple source datasets.

faq.question: What organisms are available in the Census?
faq.answer: The Census currently includes human (Homo sapiens) and mouse (Mus musculus) single-cell data.

faq.question: How do I handle queries that are too large for memory?
faq.answer: Use axis_query() with chunked table iteration instead of get_anndata(). This processes data out-of-core in batches and works for population-scale queries.
